retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Chronic Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: In the chronic phase, activated T- cells produce a variety of cytokines that down-regulate and suppress the osteoclastic activity (reducing bone resorption) and increase the production of connective tissue growth factor (TGF-beta). [7] The reduction in bone resorption and the regeneration of connective tissue structure explains why chronic lesions can remain dormant and asymptomatic for extended periods without significant radiographic changes. Histopathologically, chronic apical periodontitis lesions are encapsulated with fibrous tissue, and some may be epithelized. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Initial Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: Activated macrophages release other cytokines that intensify the vascular response and osteoclastic bone resorption. At this point, the acute phase may take different courses: healing or worsening the infection. When root canal therapy is initiated, and the offending microbes are removed from the root canal system, the lesion may resolve, and the periapical tissues will remain unchanged radiographically. [14] Conversely, if the infection is not resolved, it will become chronic, receiving the name of chronic apical periodontitis. [8] These lesions may spread through the bone to other fascial spaces causing abscesses or opening to the exterior, creating a fistula.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: As previously stated, the host response includes various cells, mediators, effector molecules, and antibodies. The cellular elements include polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMN), lymphocytes, monocytes/macrophages, and plasma cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The now infected and necrotic root canal system is a habitat that allows for the growth and establishment of a mixed, predominately anaerobic flora, which mainly grows in sessile biofilms and aggregates or co-aggregates. [6] The microbial invaders or their by-products can advance into the periapex of the root. In response, the host mounts a defensive mechanism through various cells, intracellular mediators, and humoral antibodies. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Polymorphonuclear leukocytes play a significant role in the host response, as they secrete cytoplastic granules with enzymatic properties. Although the primary responsibility of PMNs is to protect the host, they can cause structural damage to tissue cells and extracellular matrices. [11] Thus, we can see how the accumulation of PMNs plays a significant role in the destruction of periapical tissues during the acute phase of AP. [7] [11] Other host cells that play crucial roles are lymphocytes, macrophages, and osteoclasts.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Apical periodontitis is the local inflammation of the periapical tissues that originate from pulp disease. [1] It may occur due to the advancement of dental caries, trauma, or operative dental procedures. [2] The infected pulp is the main cause of apical periodontitis. The host defense response to pathogenic microbes is what triggers inflammation and the consequent destruction of periradicular tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Cysts -- Histopathology -- Periapical Cyst
retrieved_docs.content: Histologically, a periapical cyst has 1 to 2 thin cell layers of nonkeratinized stratified squamous epithelium associated with inflamed fibrous connective tissue and inflammatory infiltrates. The luminal epithelium appears “looped and arcaded” due to the inflammatory hyperplasia.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: As the invading oral microflora and the immune response clash, the periapical tissues surrounding the tooth's root, such as the periodontal ligament and alveolar bone, can be destroyed. This destruction causes periodontal lesions, which may vary in clinical manifestations. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Cysts -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Periapical cysts occur through an inflammatory process from non-vital teeth. Apical inflammation occurs due to a bacterial infection and/or pulpal necrosis, forming granulation tissue. The inflamed granulation tissue causes increased osmotic pressure, leading to the proliferation of the residual rest of Malassezia. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Thus, we can describe apical periodontitis as an inflammatory disease due to the dynamic interaction between oral bacterial invaders and the body's defensive mechanism at the tooth apex. [7] Despite the complex host defensive system, the causative organisms are well-entrenched in the intricate and delicate root canal system, which is beyond the reach of the host immune response. [8] Therefore, apical periodontitis is not self-healing, and resolution of the disease can only result through surgical or non-surgical endodontic therapy or removal of the tooth in question. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Alveolar Osteitis
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology is poorly understood, but it is generally accepted that physiologic or non-physiologic mediators trigger premature clot lysis, which ultimately results in alveolar bone exposure after exodontia. Because the literature has shown many factors significantly affect clot formation and lysis, alveolar osteitis is likely a multifactorial phenomenon. Some areas of the previous study include cytokines (specifically IL-6), several biomarkers, local intraoral bacteria, and poor oral hygiene, all of which have been implicated as potential culprits in the pathogenesis of this disease. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: A principal cause of microbial flare-ups is the apical extrusion of pulpal debris. [23] When debris is extruded through over-instrumentation and incorrect working lengths, the host's response increases inflammation to combat the extruded organisms. This acute inflammatory reaction increases vascular permeability, leading to edema. With increased hydrostatic pressure, nerve endings become compressed and generate pain. In addition, incomplete chemo-mechanical canal preparation during non-surgical endodontic therapy can cause remaining bacteria to multiply and express virulence factors, resulting in further damage to the periapical tissues. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Severe inflammatory reactions can lead to pulpal cell injury, potentially compromising pulpal vitality and survivability. Although the infection activates an immune response, immune cells and molecules are unable to effectively penetrate dentin to remove pathogens due to the narrow apical foramen of the tooth and the hard enamel surrounding the internal structures. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Initial Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: The early stage of apical periodontitis is an inflammatory process where neutrophils are extravasated to the area through chemotaxis. The neutrophils attack and kill the offending microorganisms and release leukotrienes and prostaglandins, which attract more neutrophils and macrophages to the site. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Chronic Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: If the oral microbes and their by-products remain after the initial host response, the lesion may turn from a neutrophil-dominated lesion to a macrophage-dominated lesion encapsulated in connective tissue. [7] Unlike their acute counterpart, chronic lesions are often asymptomatic. Due to the destruction of the periodontal ligament and the alveolar bone, radiographic changes become evident.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Apical periodontitis results from the encounter between microbial organisms and the host defense system. [7] Several factors influence the virulence and pathogenicity of the invading microbes, including the interactions with other microorganisms present in the root canal, the ability to evade and interfere with host cellular defenses, the release of bacterial modulins, and finally, their ability to synthesize enzymes that damage host tissues. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory morphology is recognized as an associated morphological pattern of coronary atherosclerosis and other heart disease, indicating that inflammatory changes in the arterial wall and cardiac tissue are characteristic features of these cardiovascular conditions. This relationship underscores the role of inflammation in the development and structural presentation of coronary atherosclerosis as well as a broader spectrum of heart diseases, highlighting its importance for diagnostic assessment, risk stratification, and therapeutic targeting in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 659
retrieved_docs.content: network of vascular and cardiac pathologies that share anatomical sites, inflammatory mechanisms, temporal sequences, and clinical comorbidities, informing comprehensive cardiovascular assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: الالْتِهاب (inflammation) is associated with other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities, indicating that inflammatory processes can contribute to the development or worsening of skeletal disorders and structural abnormalities of the musculoskeletal system. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as addressing underlying inflammation may help prevent or mitigate bone pathology and deformities, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: (تنكس حصوي) and choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while localized calcification around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) represents a specific form of mineral deposition. Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: atherosclerosis and other heart disease, from other and ill‑defined heart disease, and from داء تصلب الشرايين القلبي الوعائي (cardiovascular atherosclerotic disease). The term other and ill‑defined heart disease is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب (vascular dilation in the heart), providing an alternative description for this ambiguous cardiac condition. Overall, these relationships illustrate a comprehensive network in which pericardial disease (مرض تأموري) encompasses structural, inflammatory, and procedural entities; coronary atherosclerotic pathology is intertwined with inflammatory morphologies and pericarditis; and specific clinical entities such as ischemic heart disease without angina are linked to broader atherosclerotic and ill‑defined cardiac diagnoses, all of which have implications for diagnosis, classification, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 580
retrieved_docs.content: vascular change. Pericardial inflammation (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر) is clinically associated with abnormal ECG findings (مخطط كهربية القلب غير طبيعي). Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the multilingual terminology, shared inflammatory mechanisms, and hierarchical classification of arteriosclerotic and atherosclerotic heart diseases, emphasizing their relevance for accurate diagnosis, patient assessment, and comprehensive management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 236
retrieved_docs.content: (ندبة مشيمية شبكية). Hemorrhagic choroidal manifestations are noted as choroidal hemorrhage and massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي باثق). Perivascular deposits (بُقَعُ روث) are also recorded. Collectively, these entities illustrate the intersection of systemic vascular disease with specific retinal and choroidal hemorrhagic findings, neovascular disorders, inflammatory and infectious processes, traumatic and postoperative sequelae, and degenerative changes, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, therapeutic decision‑making, and prognostic assessment in ophthalmic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

Pericarditis is clinically associated with abnormal ECG findings. These interrelated classifications, clinical signs, and pharmacologic agents illustrate the hierarchical organization of acute coronary events, chronic arteriosclerotic processes, inflammatory cardiac disorders, and modern lipid‑lowering therapies, supporting precise diagnosis and targeted patient management in cardiovascular medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.
